metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the re-export shim's translation contract: clarify whether `review_code()` (when enabled) calls `produce_code_review_verdict` and translates the `code_review_verdict` → `review_result` shape, or whether `review_code()` continues to produce a `review_result` directly (with the gate path as an internal variant), and document which fields map to which.
findings.finding: The re-export shim's data-flow is under-specified: `review_code()` currently returns a `review_result` dict (via `finalize_findings`), but `produce_code_review_verdict` will return a `code_review_verdict` dict (a different shape). The plan states the shim in `code_review/__init__.py` preserves the public surface, but does not specify the translation logic — whether the shim calls `produce_code_review_verdict` and converts the verdict to `review_result`, or whether `review_code()` continues to take the old path when enabled and only emits the verdict as a side-channel.
findings.location: Section: 'Source-separate into the optional rebar.llm.code_review package'; AC: 'The public surface is preserved: rebar.llm.review_code(...) … keep names/signatures/review_result'; Scope: 'src/rebar/llm/code_review/ — the verdict finalizer + the disabled-path shim'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify `CodeReviewBatchRunner`'s constructor signature (how `diff_text`, `changed_files`, `base`, `head` are injected), its `BatchRunner.run()` override contract, and how its output maps to `code_review_verdict` findings — or explicitly state it delegates to the existing `review_code` inner machinery instead of a new BatchRunner subclass.
findings.finding: `CodeReviewBatchRunner` is referenced as a concrete class to construct in `produce_code_review_verdict`, but this class does not exist in the codebase and its interface is never specified: the plan gives no definition of its constructor API (how diff context is injected — `base`, `head`, `diff_text`, `changed_files`), no `BatchRunner` subclass contract, and no description of how it differs from `ProductionBatchRunner`. An executor who reads only the plan cannot implement this class.
findings.location: ## What — 'wiring the CodeReviewBatchRunner (constructed with the assembled diff context)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `gates/code-review.yaml` to the Scope as an explicit deliverable and specify its top-level structure: inputs (diff_text, changed_files, base, head), steps (at minimum: a findings step, a terminal verdict-assembly step), and how the CodeReviewBatchRunner is wired in as the batch runner.
findings.finding: `gates/code-review.yaml` is referenced as the workflow `produce_code_review_verdict` runs in-memory, but this file does not exist and its structure is never authored in the plan: no step IDs, no inputs declaration, no batch step with criteria, no terminal step, no branch logic. The parallel `gates/plan-review.yaml` is a fully authored 150+ line spec; code-review has nothing equivalent.
findings.location: ## What — 'running WS3's gates/code-review.yaml in-memory'; ## Scope — gate_dispatch.py

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the PASS/BLOCK decision rule for the code-review verdict finalizer: what condition(s) produce BLOCK vs PASS (e.g. any finding in `blocking[]` → BLOCK; empty blocking → PASS; or a severity/posture threshold), and whether a third state (INDETERMINATE/ERROR) is possible on LLM failure.
findings.finding: The `code_review_verdict` schema defines `verdict: 'PASS'|'BLOCK'` but the decision rule for how the gate produces PASS vs BLOCK is never specified. `plan_review_verdict` has `finalize_verdict` with a multi-case decision tree documented in the plan and the code (DET block → BLOCK; llm_unavailable → INDETERMINATE; verify_failed → conditional; else PASS). The code-review equivalent has no equivalent 'verdict finalizer' decision rule — what makes a code-review verdict BLOCK? Severity threshold? Any blocking finding? The schema says only two values exist, but the rule assigning them is absent.
findings.location: ## code_review_verdict schema; ## Scope — 'the verdict finalizer'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify `CodeReviewBatchRunner`'s contract for the no-`target_ticket` / diff-only path: does it ignore `target_ticket`, require a `BatchRunRequest` change, or use a different seam entirely — and reconcile with the frozen `BatchRunRequest.target_ticket: str | None` field and `ProductionBatchRunner`'s guard.
findings.finding: The plan names `CodeReviewBatchRunner` as a first-class component but doesn't specify how it handles the `BatchRunRequest.target_ticket` contract: `ProductionBatchRunner.run()` raises `ValueError` when `target_ticket` is `None`, and `BatchRunRequest` is a frozen dataclass with `target_ticket: str | None`. A diff-only code review has no ticket, so it's unverified whether the new runner suppresses the guard, ignores `target_ticket` entirely, or requires a structural change to `BatchRunRequest`.
findings.location: ## What — 'wiring the `CodeReviewBatchRunner` (constructed with the assembled diff context)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a step-structure sketch for `gates/code-review.yaml` to the plan (at minimum: which step ids produce `verdict`, `blocking`, `advisory`; whether it has a precheck short-circuit; and whether the recover-on-failure helpers from `gate_dispatch` are reused or replaced).
findings.finding: `gates/code-review.yaml` does not exist in the repo (`src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/` contains only `completion-verification.yaml` and `plan-review.yaml`) and the plan provides no step-structure spec for it. Without the gate YAML's step layout, the claim that `CodeReviewBatchRunner` + `RunnerAgentStep` + `MemoryRecorder` compose correctly via `produce_code_review_verdict` is an unverified capability assertion — the executor's `batch` step dispatch, `branch` wiring, and output-schema resolution all depend on the YAML structure.
findings.location: ## What — '`produce_code_review_verdict` running WS3's `gates/code-review.yaml` in-memory'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either define `CodeReviewBatchRunner` as an explicit WS4 deliverable in the Scope section (with its adapter contract: how does it receive the assembled diff context and map it to `BatchRunRequest`?), or clarify whether the code-review gate uses an `agent` step rather than a `batch` construct — in which case the plan text must be corrected to remove the `CodeReviewBatchRunner` reference.
findings.finding: CodeReviewBatchRunner is named as the batch runner to wire into produce_code_review_verdict but does not exist anywhere in the codebase.
findings.location: ## What — 'wiring the CodeReviewBatchRunner (constructed with the assembled diff context)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecar schema strategy: specify the schema key for code-review payloads and state explicitly whether `latest_review_result()` is extended to recognise it, or whether code-review and plan-review sidecars are distinguishable on a shared ticket.
findings.finding: The plan reuses the `REVIEW_RESULT` event type for the code-review sidecar, but `sidecar.build_payload()` hardcodes `"schema": "plan_review_result_v1"` and `latest_review_result()` silently skips any payload whose `schema` field doesn't equal `"plan_review_result_v1"`. A code-review sidecar written with the same event type but a different schema key (or the same key with incompatible fields) will be invisibly ignored by the existing reader — or will be mis-interpreted as a plan-review payload.
findings.location: ## Sidecar mechanism — 'a thin `code_review` sidecar emit reuses the `REVIEW_RESULT` event TYPE (not a new format)'
findings.suggested_fix: Use a distinct schema key (e.g. `'code_review_result_v1'`) for the code-review sidecar payload and add a parallel `latest_code_review_result()` reader, rather than routing through `latest_review_result()` which guards on the plan-review schema key.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether `src/rebar/llm/code_review/` (as a package directory with `__init__.py`) was actually created in WS1-WS3; if not, add the flat-module-to-package migration step explicitly to the WS4 Scope section so its effort is visible.
findings.finding: The plan states 'the optional rebar.llm.code_review package (already created in WS1-WS3)' but only a flat code_review.py module exists; no code_review/ package directory is present.
findings.location: ## What — 'Source-separate into the optional rebar.llm.code_review package (already created in WS1-WS3)'; ## Scope — 'src/rebar/llm/code_review/'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the code-review sidecar's `schema` tag (e.g. `'code_review_result_v1'`) and clarify whether `sidecar.latest_review_result` must be updated to skip it (the reader's `schema == 'plan_review_result_v1'` guard already isolates plan-review reads) or whether the code-review path uses a separate emit function that never shares a file path with plan-review sidecars.
findings.finding: The sidecar emit for code-review reuses the `REVIEW_RESULT` event TYPE but the existing `sidecar.build_payload` writes `schema: 'plan_review_result_v1'` and `sidecar.latest_review_result` skips payloads whose `schema != 'plan_review_result_v1'`. The plan says 'a thin code_review sidecar emit reuses the REVIEW_RESULT event TYPE (not a new format)' without specifying the payload schema tag or how the code-review-specific fields (blocking/advisory/coaching/coverage) map to the existing payload structure — creating ambiguity about whether a code-review sidecar would be accidentally consumed or rejected by the plan-review sidecar reader.
findings.location: ## Sidecar mechanism — 'a thin code_review sidecar emit reuses the REVIEW_RESULT event TYPE (not a new format)'
findings.suggested_fix: Use a distinct `schema: 'code_review_result_v1'` tag in the code-review sidecar payload; the existing `latest_review_result` guard already rejects non-plan-review payloads, so no change to the plan-review reader is needed. Update `_NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES` to recognize the code-review event. Document this in the plan's Sidecar section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `gates/code-review.yaml` is a WS3 deliverable that already ships or a WS4 deliverable; if the latter, add it explicitly to the Scope section and verify `_gate_doc('code-review', repo_root)` resolves correctly before wiring the dispatch function.
findings.finding: gates/code-review.yaml does not exist in the codebase; produce_code_review_verdict cannot run without it.
findings.location: ## What — 'running WS3's gates/code-review.yaml in-memory'; ## Scope — 'src/rebar/llm/workflow/gate_dispatch.py — produce_code_review_verdict'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/code-review.yaml` to the Scope section. The plan already lists the file as the gate driver; it must be a named deliverable so reviewers and the author can verify it will exist before `produce_code_review_verdict` is wired.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a failure-mode section to `produce_code_review_verdict` specifying what the function returns (or raises) when the `gates/code-review.yaml` workflow fails mid-run — e.g. whether it degrades to an inert/disabled-equivalent result, propagates the LLMUnavailableError, or returns a BLOCK-equivalent verdict.
findings.finding: The enabled-path failure-recovery semantics for `produce_code_review_verdict` are unspecified. `produce_plan_review_verdict` has three explicit fallback paths (coach failure recovery, verify-step failure recovery, systemic-outage degradation to INDETERMINATE). The WS4 plan describes only the happy-path and the disabled path; it does not specify what `produce_code_review_verdict` returns when the `gates/code-review.yaml` workflow fails mid-run (e.g. an LLM-tier outage, a verifier step exhausting its budget).
findings.location: Section: 'produce_code_review_verdict runs gates/code-review.yaml on the v3 engine'; AC line: 'produce_code_review_verdict returns a well-formed code_review_verdict'; Back-out section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the fallback/degradation protocol for `produce_code_review_verdict` when the workflow fails mid-run (e.g. LLMUnavailableError → return inert disabled result; workflow not-succeeded → return BLOCK or raise; batch runner failure → return empty verdict with error note) so the decision rule is co-located with the dispatch spec.
findings.finding: No fallback/degradation protocol is specified for `produce_code_review_verdict` mid-run failures. `produce_plan_review_verdict` has three explicit recovery paths (_recover_plan_review_coach_failure, _recover_plan_review_verify_failure, _degraded_plan_review_verdict) each with a documented decision rule. The code-review dispatch specifies only the disabled-path inert result; if the workflow fails mid-run (LLMUnavailableError, BatchRunner failure, workflow not-succeeded), the fallback behavior is undefined.
findings.location: ## What — produce_code_review_verdict description; ## Back-out
findings.suggested_fix: Given the diff-only path has no ticket anchor, the simplest specified fallback is: any LLMUnavailableError or workflow failure → return the same inert disabled result (zero findings, 'llm_unavailable' note in coverage), matching the disabled-path shape. State this explicitly in the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the error-exit shape for the enabled path on the CLI/MCP surface (e.g., a structured error result or a caught exception with a user-facing message), and add at least one AC covering a gate-failure or timeout scenario.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify how the `rebar review-code` CLI and MCP `review_code` tool handle errors in the enabled path (LLM timeout, gate workflow failure, malformed verdict from the runner). The disabled path returns a clean empty result, but the enabled-path error exit is unspecified, risking unhandled exceptions surfaced raw to users.
findings.location: Scope section / AC: 'produce_code_review_verdict runs gates/code-review.yaml … and returns the typed code_review_verdict'; src/rebar/_cli/_llm_commands.py, src/rebar/mcp_server.py

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/config.py` to the Scope list and specify that a `CodeReviewConfig` dataclass (with `enabled: bool = False`) must be registered in `_SECTION_CLASSES` and `_SECTIONS` so the `[code_review]` TOML section is recognized and coerced correctly.
findings.finding: `src/rebar/config.py` is absent from the Scope list, but the plan introduces `[code_review] enabled = false` as a `rebar.toml` config section. The existing `Config` dataclass in `config.py` uses a typed `_SECTION_CLASSES` registry; a new `[code_review]` TOML section requires adding a `CodeReviewConfig` dataclass, registering it in `_SECTION_CLASSES` and `_SECTIONS`, and adding it as a field on `Config`. Without this, `rebar.toml`-based `enabled=false` will be silently ignored or raise a warning on unknown-section.
findings.location: Section: 'Feature flag'; Scope list (omission); src/rebar/config.py
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: 'src/rebar/config.py — add CodeReviewConfig(enabled: bool = False) dataclass, register in _SECTION_CLASSES["code_review"] and add the enabled bool coercer to _SECTIONS["code_review"]'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one failure/invalid-input AC for the enabled path: e.g., `produce_code_review_verdict` with an empty diff returns a well-formed inert result (or a structured error), AND with a runner that raises an exception the dispatch propagates a typed error rather than an uncaught exception; exercise the same in the public-surface test (AC6).
findings.finding: The enabled-path tests for `produce_code_review_verdict` (AC2) cover only the happy path (well-formed diff → valid verdict). No AC or test plan addresses failure/error/empty paths: e.g., malformed or empty diff input, gate YAML execution error/timeout, schema validation failure on the returned verdict, or sidecar emit failure. This is a new user-facing flow exposed via CLI (`rebar review-code`) and MCP (`review_code`), so failure paths are required by E5.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 (ENABLED-state offline test) and AC6 (public surface test)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC2: 'A FakeRunner configured to raise on the empty-diff case verifies that produce_code_review_verdict returns a structured error dict (or raises a documented exception type) rather than propagating a raw engine error.' Add to AC6: 'The MCP review_code tool, when called with an empty/malformed diff, returns a structured error response, not an unhandled exception.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Rewrite AC8 as an outcome-framed, mechanically verifiable criterion — e.g. 'No file under docs/ or CLI --help output contains a reference to single_pass.py or the retired evidence+aggregate_findings-only route; enforced by a grep/CI check or a docs-review note in the ADR.'
findings.finding: AC8 ('stale references … are audited + updated') is process/effort-framed rather than outcome-framed, leaving it unclear what 'done' looks like — an agent cannot deterministically verify completion.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC8: 'Stale references to the single-pass code_review.py/single_pass.py flow in docs / CLI-help / docs/ are audited + updated.'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'are audited + updated' with: 'contain no surviving references to single_pass.py or the retired evidence+aggregate_findings-only route (verified by grep in CI or a documented docs-review checklist committed alongside the ADR).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a documentation deliverable (README section, config reference page, or docs/ entry) for `[code_review] enabled` / `REBAR_CODE_REVIEW_ENABLED`, and add a corresponding AC or extend AC8 to cover both stale-removal audits and new-config additions.
findings.finding: The new `[code_review] enabled` config key and `REBAR_CODE_REVIEW_ENABLED` environment variable are user-facing operator controls with no planned user-facing documentation update (README, config reference, or docs/ page), creating a stale-by-omission gap for operators who need to enable the feature.
findings.location: Feature flag section; Scope section (docs/adr/ and stale-reference audit)

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align AC7 to a single canonical term for the retired route (e.g. 'the `single_pass` execution path') and specify the concrete verification step (e.g. 'module is deleted AND the enabled gate never calls `aggregate_findings`).'
findings.finding: AC7 names the removed entity two different ways — 'single-pass GATE PATH' and 'no `evidence + aggregate_findings`-only route runs when enabled' — for the same concept, causing terminology drift that could confuse test implementors about what exactly to assert is unreachable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC7
findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite AC7 as: 'The `single_pass` execution path is removed (`single_pass.py` deleted) and unreachable when the gate is enabled (the enabled path never calls `aggregate_findings`); an ADR records the removal.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether the ticket-scoped sidecar emit is idempotent (upsert/deduplication by ticket+revision key) or append-only, and document the expected behavior under retry/concurrent calls.
findings.finding: The sidecar event-store write in the ticket-scoped path has no stated idempotency or deduplication guarantee. If `produce_code_review_verdict` is called twice for the same `target_ticket` (e.g., a retry or a concurrent Gerrit webhook), two `REVIEW_RESULT` events will accumulate in the event store for that ticket, corrupting the event log.
findings.location: Sidecar mechanism section / AC: 'a target_ticket-scoped call emits a REVIEW_RESULT sidecar on that ticket'
findings.suggested_fix: State explicitly that the emit uses an upsert keyed on (ticket_id, event_type='REVIEW_RESULT', source='code_review'), matching plan-review's pattern, so retries are safe. If append-only is intentional, document that callers must deduplicate at read time.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Revise the sequencing to make the gate-dispatch integration the first gated checkpoint: complete and test `produce_code_review_verdict` + schema validation (AC1, AC2, AC5) as a passing mini-milestone BEFORE adding sidecar emit (AC4), source-separation shim, and docs/ADR work — so engine-wiring bugs surface before downstream scope is built.
findings.finding: The intra-WS4 sequencing ('register the schema → add the verdict finalizer + dispatch → wire the flag + disabled shim → retire single_pass + audit docs') is a horizontal layered build rather than a thin vertical slice. The riskiest integration — that `CodeReviewBatchRunner + RunnerAgentStep + MemoryRecorder` are correctly wired in `produce_code_review_verdict` and that the gate YAML produces a schema-valid verdict — is not de-risked first with a minimal end-to-end slice before the sidecar, docs audit, and ADR scope is added. If the engine wiring is wrong, all subsequent sidecar/docs work is built on an unvalidated foundation.
findings.location: Rationale section — 'Sequencing within WS4: register the schema → add the verdict finalizer + dispatch (enabled path) → wire the flag + disabled shim → retire single_pass + audit docs'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
